description: The second option is to hike down from Helong Park, the first stop on our route. This trail takes about 2.5 hours and leads to the , a beautiful area surrounded by stunning sandstone spires. Hiking here allows you to admire the formations from the ground rather than from above.

Here, you will also find one of the most popular photo spots, the , where you can get that iconic photo of the karst mountains with the river as featured on the 20 CNY banknote. There is a dedicated spot to take that photo, but you have to pay for it. Don't, as you can get the exact same view next to it or from the balcony located on the second floor of the pier for free.

One of the weirdest and most surprising attractions I visited in Zhangjiajie is , also known as the 72 Wonder Towers, a large cultural and tourism complex where you can see all kinds of lights and sounds shows throughout the day. It is a great place to visit at night if you are looking for a place to hang out in Zhangjiajie after sundown.

While at the Middle Station, be sure to look over the cliff and check out , a winding mountain road that stretches over 10 km and rises from roughly 200 meters above sea level to about 1,300 meters, with a total elevation gain of a whopping 1,100 meters.

A little further south from Mandarin's House, you will find , one of Macau’s oldest and most famous temples, dating back to 1488. The temple has several shrines at different levels along the rocky hillside, so be sure to hike all the way to the shrine at the top!

For dinner, I highly recommend trying the fish ball noodles from . They have several branches around Hong Kong, and I visited the one near my hotel in Fortress Hill, and it was great. Find the branch closest to your accommodation and give it a try before calling it a day.

From Ashun, you will find regular tourist buses running from  to Huangguoshu Scenic Area. The ride takes about 40–50 minutes and costs around 25 CNY one-way. You can also use Didi or find a carpool to take you there for around 85 - 100 CNY and 35 CNY respectively.
From Guiyang, you can take a high-speed train from Guiyang North Railway Station to , which should take about 30 minutes and cost 47 CNY. You can book the train here.

You can also stop by  for lunch, which is one of my favorite local restaurants located not too far from Senado Square, tucked away in a basement of a small street, and they serve all kinds of local dishes like spicy beef noodles, brisket sandwiches, egg tarts, and more.

After taking photos of the reclining Buddha image, you can turn left along the river to visit , where you will be surrounded by countless Buddha carvings and sculptures. You can continue through the cave and follow the route until you arrive at another cave, where all the cool Buddha images are, as well as a connection to the Giant Buddha you are here to see.

After a short ride from Swan Lake, you will arrive at  and its beautiful waterfall. The lake sits at an altitude of about 2,629 m and covers roughly 151,000 m². The lake is relatively shallow (around 5–10 m deep) and is known for its stable water level throughout the year; it doesn’t fully freeze even in winter.

There are countless viewpoints along the way, and you could easily spend at least an hour or two exploring Yuanjiajie Mountain alone. When you reach the end of the trail, you have a few options for getting down. The fastest way is to take the , which is quick but often crowded. However, this means you might miss one of the best parts of Zhangjiajie: the peaceful trails where you can hike down and escape the crowds.

Lijiang Old Town is quite commercialized and if you prefer a more rustic and authentic looking old town, I highly recommend you come and check out , one of the oldest Naxi settlements in the region located about 20 minutes' drive north of Lijiang Old Town. It was the political and cultural center of the Naxi people for centuries, and served as the base for the Mu chieftain family long before the rise of Dayan (Lijiang) as the dominant town.

Another fun activity you can do in Yangshuo is to hire a  and explore the Yulong River, which is smaller than the Li River and a bit more peaceful. There are several routes you can take but the most popular one is from Shui'edi Pier to Gongnong Bridge, which I recommend you do.

To take the cruise along the river to see the Giant Leshan Buddha, you can buy the ticket and board the boat from . The boat operates from 7:30 AM to 6:30 PM, and it will take you about 30 minutes with a photo stop at the Giant Leshan Buddha for you to take photos. It should cost around 80 CNY per person, and you can get the ticket from the wharf.

The last stop is . The bus will need to backtrack to the entrance and take another route around the mountain, so be sure not to get off at the first stop. Once you arrive, you will walk along an elevated walkway for about 15 minutes to reach the lake.

One of the most photogenic viewpoints near Lijiang Old Town that you shouldn't miss is from , a beautiful park famous for its crystal-clear lake that perfectly reflects the Jade Dragon Snow Mountain on clear days.
As you explore Yulong Snow Mountain, be sure to also visit , located at the foot of the mountain. You can easily reach there from Yulong Cable Car Station via a free shuttle bus inside the park.

By Plane: Lijiang Sanyi International Airport (LJG) is the main gateway into the city. Direct flights operate from major Chinese cities such as Kunming, Chengdu, Chongqing, Guangzhou, Beijing, and Shanghai. From the airport, it takes about 30 to 40 minutes to reach Lijiang Old Town by taxi or DiDi. There is also an airport shuttle bus that runs to Lijiang Old Town and .

Located at an altitude of 3,320 meters, you will find , another cool spot you can check out while hiking up the Huanglong Scenic Area. The pond embankment follows the contours of the tree roots and the natural terrain, with the embankment connecting seamlessly to the banks and the flowing water, which cascades in layered terraces.
Accommodation: Trip.com, and  are the ultimate go-to platforms to search for amazing hostels and hotels at affordable prices.

One of the highest areas you can explore to acclimatize yourself with the high altitude of Dali is , a majestic mountain range rising over 4,000 meters above sea level, located just west of Dali City. You can easily access it via the cable car station.

By Bus: Before the high-speed train, you used to have to take the grueling 8 - 10 hour bus ride from Chengdu to visit Huanglong and Jiuzhaigou. Well, you can still do it today if you are looking to save a bit of money and have time to spare. The buses depart from  and take around 8 to 10 hours, depending on traffic and road conditions. Make sure when you buy your ticket that the bus goes to Songpan, not Jiuzhaigou, if you want to start in Huanglong. Once you arrive in Songpan, you can take a taxi for another hour to get to Huanglong. Personally, I wouldn't recommend it, but at least you know you have the option.

By Bus: Before the high-speed train, you used to have to take the grueling 8 - 10 hour bus ride from Chengdu to visit Jiuzhaigou. Well, you can still do it today if you are looking to save a bit of money and have time to spare. The buses depart from Xinnanmen Bus Station or  and take around 8 to 10 hours, depending on traffic and road conditions. Make sure when you buy your ticket that the bus goes all the way to Jiuzhaigou Valley, not just to Jiuzhaigou County, which is further away. I wouldn't recommend it personally, but at least you know you have the option.

By High-Speed Train: Another excellent way to reach Chengdu is by high-speed train. The city is well connected to China’s extensive rail network, making train travel fast, comfortable, and scenic. High-speed trains from Chongqing take around one hour, from Xi’an about three hours, and from Beijing or Shanghai roughly eight to ten hours. Trains arrive at one of several major stations, including  and Chengdu South, both of which are linked to the metro system.

By High-Speed Train: High-speed trains depart from , Chengdu South, and Chengdu West Stations, arriving at Leshan Railway Station. Once you arrive, you can take a local bus or taxi to the Leshan Giant Buddha Scenic Area. A Didi ride takes about 20 minutes, while the bus takes around 40 minutes depending on traffic.

To get to Leshan, you can easily catch one of the many trains from  to Leshan Station. The journey takes about 45 minutes and costs around 65 CNY. You can book the train here. Unlike Jiuzhaigou, you do not need to book in advance, as there are plenty of trains throughout the day and the site does not get as crowded. Entry tickets can be purchased at the entrance or online here.

By Train: One of the most popular ways to get to Huanglong is to take a high-speed train from Chengdu. You can take a train from  to Huanglong–Jiuzhai Railway Station, which takes about two hours. Be sure to book your train tickets together with your Jiuzhaigou entrance ticket at least three days in advance if you are planning to go to Jiuzhaigou as well, as both sell out very quickly. You do not want to secure your park ticket but then find that the trains are fully booked, leaving you without a way to get there and back.

Right next to Tianfu Square, you will find , a history museum opened in 2016, famous for its huge collection of nearly 200,000 relics and cultural objects, displayed in a way to help you trace the history of Chengdu and Sichuan from the Neolithic age all the way to modern times.

By High-Speed Train: Another excellent way to reach Chengdu is by high-speed train. The city is well connected to China’s extensive rail network, making train travel fast, comfortable, and scenic. High-speed trains from Chongqing take around one hour, from Xi’an about three hours, and from Beijing or Shanghai roughly eight to ten hours. Trains arrive at one of several major stations, including Chengdu East and , both of which are linked to the metro system.

By High-Speed Train: High-speed trains depart from Chengdu East, , and Chengdu West Stations, arriving at Leshan Railway Station. Once you arrive, you can take a local bus or taxi to the Leshan Giant Buddha Scenic Area. A Didi ride takes about 20 minutes, while the bus takes around 40 minutes depending on traffic.

By Air: Chengdu is one of the major transportation hubs in western China, so getting here is easy whether you are traveling domestically or internationally. Most travelers arrive by air through , the city’s main gateway.

By Air: Chengdu is the place I recommend you stay while you make a day trip to visit Leshan. Getting to Chengdu is easy since it is one of the major transportation hubs in western China, and you should be able to find flights here whether you are traveling domestically or internationally. Most travelers arrive by air through , the city’s main gateway.

By High-Speed Train: High-speed trains depart from Chengdu East, Chengdu South, and , arriving at Leshan Railway Station. Once you arrive, you can take a local bus or taxi to the Leshan Giant Buddha Scenic Area. A Didi ride takes about 20 minutes, while the bus takes around 40 minutes depending on traffic.

By Bus: Direct buses also run from  to Leshan, but they are slower and less convenient. The ride usually takes 2 hours or more, so this option is only recommended if train tickets are sold out.

If you have time to spare and prefer to acclimatize as you go as well as save some money, you can take a long and winding overland journey from Chengdu to Daocheng. The bus typically leaves at 6:30 AM from  to Daocheng and should take about 12 - 15 hours. Once in Daocheng, you can switch to a second bus to Shangri-La Town. Be sure to confirm the time and get the ticket the day prior.

After resting for a bit, you can walk to  and explore the temple grounds. It is a beautiful Tibetan-style monastery with a stunning courtyard where you can see the iconic white exterior of the monastery set against the snow-capped mountains of Yading. This is also where you can begin your last hike in Yading, the hike to Pearl Lake.

place: Chongsheng Temple and the Three Pagoda Cultural Area
description: One of the most iconic landmarks in Dali is , located just a short Didi ride from Dali Ancient Town. This historic complex is famous for its three ancient independent pagodas, arranged in an equilateral triangle, which are among the oldest and best-preserved in Yunnan.

is Chengdu’s most famous shopping street and a must-visit for those looking to experience the city’s modern vibe. Located in the city center, reachable by the metros, it is a bustling pedestrian street lined with shopping malls, international and local brands, boutiques, and plenty of street food stalls you can enjoy.

One of the most beautiful spots in Dukezong Ancient Town is , a large Tibetan Buddhist temple built on top of Guishan Park (Turtle Hill Park) overlooking the entire city. It is the perfect place to begin your exploration of the ancient town and also get a panoramic view of the city from above.

After Helong Park, you can head over to the same shuttle bus stop and get on another one to go to Yangjiajie Mountain. After about 20 minutes, the shuttle bus will stop first at , which is where all the accommodations inside the park are. Do not get off here as there is not much to see except a few accommodations and maybe one or two viewpoints that are not particularly impressive.

By Plane: Dali has its own airport, , located about 30 minutes from Dali City. There are regular flights from major Chinese cities like Kunming, Chengdu, Guangzhou, and Beijing. From the airport, you can take a Didi or airport shuttle to Dali City or nearby towns like Dali Ancient Town.

The park is open from 8:30 AM to 10 PM daily, and the entry fee is 38 CNY per person. You can buy the ticket at the entrance or get it online here. There is also a shuttle bus that costs 4 CNY departing from  departing between 9 AM and 4 PM daily, and you can book the shuttle bus here.

Of course, when you are in Dali, you gotta visit , a town with a long and rich history having served as the capital of both the Nanzhao Kingdom and the Dali Kingdom. Today, the place has been converted into a commercialized town like many other ancient villages in China, but it still preserves much of its traditional Bai architecture which is wonderful to explore.

This Bai ethnic village is located remotely in the mountains, which is why it still retains its authenticity. The only way to get there is by road, which is about 2-3 hours by car from Dali Ancient Town or 4-5 hours by public buses and minivan. These leave from , and you will have to transfer to a minivan in Jianchuan.

By Train: Dali is accessible by high-speed and regular trains. The  is well connected to Kunming and other parts of Yunnan. For those who are already in the southern part of China, this is the best way to get here. You can book the train here.

In case there happens to be a taxi waiting at the airport, you might be able to catch one to , where you can transfer to the bus to Shangri-La (Riwa). The bus typically departs twice a day, around 10:00 and 14:00. Keep in mind that schedules can change and taxis may not always be available at the airport. Personally, I wouldn’t risk relying on this option just to save a few bucks.

The fastest and most convenient way is to fly into , one of the highest airports in the world at 4,411 meters above sea level. You can find direct flights from major cities like Chengdu, Chongqing, and Kunming. The flight should take between 1 - 2 hours and cost around 261 USD. You can book the flight here.

By Air: The quickest way to arrive is by flying into , located about 5 km from the city center. There are direct flights from major cities like Kunming, Lijiang, Chengdu, Chongqing, Guangzhou, and sometimes Beijing or Shanghai depending on the season. Once you land, taxis and airport buses can take you into town in around 15 minutes.

After you are done with the Grand Waterfall Area, you can take a shuttle bus to , which is located downstream towards the entrance of the park. This is a smaller, but equally charming waterfall within the Huangguoshu Scenic Area and you can walk on its elevated path that runs along the river all the way to the waterfall.

is the ancient old town of Shangri-La that dates back more than 1,300 years, with origins traced as far back as the Tang dynasty. Historically, Dukezong was an important stop on the Ancient Tea Horse Road, the trade route that connected Yunnan with Tibet and beyond.

You can start your visit from the , the oldest and most popular of the three remaining gates. From there, you can climb the tower and access the old walls. Once on the walls, you can enjoy views of the cobblestone streets below, which are especially enchanting in the evening. Entry to the tower itself costs 5 CNY, but walking along the walls does not require a fee.

To help you navigate the park more easily, I am going to list everything in the order I visited, so you can follow along as we make our way through the park. We’ll begin by taking the cable car up to Tianzi Mountain, which you can do right after entering the .

First and foremost, be sure to get the entry ticket and the cable car ticket prior to your arrival at the gate so you can scan your passport and go right in. You can book both tickets from here. Since Wulingyuan is on the , you can walk there in about 5 minutes and be the first to enter the park.

is one of my favorite areas to visit in Chengdu. It is a former industrial zone, once home to the state‑owned Hongguang Electronic Tube Factory, that has been transformed into one of Chengdu’s most creative and cultural hotspots.

Another popular, albeit extremely crowded, attraction you should also check out while you are in Guilin City is , one of Guilin’s most famous landmarks, which is named after its distinctive shape that resembles an elephant dipping its trunk into the Li River. It is located right next to Sun and Moon Pagodas, so naturally, it is a great place to drop by.

One of the main highlights of visiting Dali is experiencing the beautiful , which feels more like being in Europe than in China. This stunning freshwater lake stretches approximately 42 kilometers from north to south and about 7 kilometers at its widest point. It is the second-largest highland lake in Yunnan and is a key feature that makes Dali stand out from other places in the province.

On the opposite side, you will find the , which faces east and is a perfect spot to catch a stunning sunrise if you arrive early enough. Many photographers gather here in the morning to capture the dramatic sunrise views.

Due to its close proximity to Zhangjiajie, many travelers visiting the area also stop by , a wonderful riverside ancient city, for a night before or after their trip to Zhangjiajie. Here, you can explore the beautiful wooden stilt houses, take a peaceful walk along the river, and admire the ancient city at night when the entire river is illuminated with lanterns and lights.

On your third day, you can start a bit later to recover from the previous night before taking the high-speed train from Zhangjiajie West Railway Station to , a charming riverside town just about 25 minutes away. Here, you can explore the beautiful wooden stilt houses, enjoy a peaceful walk along the river, and admire the city at night when the entire riverside is illuminated with lanterns and lights.
The town is served by China's high-speed rail network via , and you should be able to get here from most major cities like Zhangjiajie City, Chengdu, Chongqing, and more.

To return, you can head back to the square and check the schedule for the next shuttle. However, if you stay late to enjoy the lights at night, it may be best to use Didi to get to  so you do not miss your train back.

Next up is , the most beautiful multi-colored lake in Jiuzhaigou in my opinion, and definitely the highlight of my trip here. It sits at an altitude of about 2,472 m. The lake is relatively shallow (around 5 m deep) and covers roughly 90,000 m². Its magical, multicolored water comes from a combination of calcium‑carbonate deposits, algae, and submerged water plants.

Afterwards, you can continue along the cliffside path, taking in stunning views such as the  and many others. One of my favorite viewpoints here is the Hallelujah Mountain, which inspired the dreamy landscapes in the movie Avatar. The solitary vertical pillar rising amidst the picturesque scenery is truly a sight to behold.

It took me about 45 minutes to hike the steep section before arriving at the Qujiuzhaga Rest Stop and an intersection where you can choose to visit Milk Lake or . The trails are connected in a triangle shape, so you can choose either way and continue to the next one before the trail loops back to this intersection.

From the temple, it is only a few minutes' walk to the , arguably the most beautiful attraction in Huanglong Scenic Park. The pool sits high in the valley at about 3,576 meters above sea level and is considered the largest and highest outdoor travertine pond cluster in the world. It is also one of the most surreal sights in China, a place that made me wonder, "How is this even real?"

From Yunmeng Fairy Peak, you can either hike the longer route to Tianmen Temple or take the , an open-air, ski-style cable car that lets your feet dangle over cliffs and mountains. Riding it is an exciting experience in itself, in my opinion, and I highly recommend it.

One of the most iconic features of Tianmen Mountain, which is probably the main reason why many travelers decided to visit, is the natural arch carved into the cliff face called Tianmen Cave, or popularly known as the “”. This enormous rock‑arch stands about 131.5 metres tall and around 57 metres wide, making it one of the largest natural arches at high altitude in the world.

If you prefer to skip the Oriental Buddha Park, you can take a taxi directly to the  and pay only 80 CNY for the Giant Buddha ticket. You can also buy tickets online here before your arrival. Be sure to bring your passport when purchasing or collecting tickets.

Of course, when you visit Chengdu, one of the few pandas' natural habitats, you gotta come and see the Giant Pandas with your own eyes, and one of the best places to do so is at , located about 10 kilometers north of downtown Chengdu.

Another exciting activity is walking the , a transparent walkway that clings to the cliff face, allowing you to look straight down at the valleys hundreds of meters below. It’s definitely not for the faint of heart.

As you follow the winding boardwalk upward, you will come across the , a stunning cascade where water tumbles down a rocky ledge about 10 meters high and 60 meters wide. It forms dozens of tiered waterfalls that shimmer like silver pearls rolling down, resembling a sparkling curtain of water suspended high above.

For those who want to hike down, make sure you have at least 2–3 hours to spare, as it will take that long to descend the mountain. You can choose between two trails. One is the , a beautiful path that follows a peaceful river, with pavilions along the way where you can rest. This route starts right at the end of Yuanjiajie Mountain, making it perfect if you are following my suggested route beginning with the cable car.

From Luorong Cattle Farm, you have the option to begin hiking the main trail or hire a horse for 300 CNY per person to take you to , which is roughly halfway to Milk Lake and Five-Color Lake. In my opinion, this is unnecessary, as the trail from the cattle farm to  is flat, scenic, and enjoyable, and you would miss much of it if you take the horse.

First and foremost, I recommend starting your visit at the , home to the crown jewel of the park, the magnificent Huangguoshu Waterfall. After purchasing your ticket and entering the scenic area, take the shuttle bus from the "Entrance to the  Carriage Way" bus stop, which is located on the right-hand side.

The shuttle bus will drop you off at Tianqiao Bus Stop and from there, you can begin exploring Yuanjiajie Mountain starting with the , a rocky natural bridge that connects 2 mountain peaks together. On the peak, you will also find a temple where you can pay respect and enjoy all the viewpoints around the area.

The shuttle bus will drop you off at Tianqiao Bus Stop, and from there, you can begin exploring Yuanjiajie Mountain starting with the , a rocky natural bridge that connects 2 mountain peaks together with a vertical drop of a whopping 400 meters!
From Guangzhou, you can take a high-speed train from  to Guilin North Railway Station. It costs around 20 USD and takes about 3 hours. You can book the ticket in advance here.

After spending almost a week here, here’s my recommendation on where to stay in Guilin. First and foremost, you’ll need to get into  via high-speed train and stay at least one night. If you plan to visit the Longji Rice Terrace, consider staying two nights so you can take a day trip the next day. My recommended accommodations in  are listed below:

This itinerary begins in , which is well-connected by China’s high-speed rail network. High-speed trains run from major cities like Guangzhou, Shenzhen, Chengdu, and Shanghai, with travel times ranging from a few hours to overnight depending on your departure point.

By Plane:  is the main gateway for flyers. The airport has frequent domestic flights from major cities like Beijing, Shanghai, Guangzhou, and Chengdu, as well as a few direct international flights from countries such as Thailand, Singapore, Malaysia, and South Korea. From the airport, it’s about 30 km to Guilin city center. You can take a taxi, which usually takes around 40–50 minutes, or hop on an airport shuttle bus for a more budget-friendly option.

By High Speed Train: Guilin is also well-connected by China’s high-speed rail network. High-speed trains arrive from cities like Guangzhou, Shenzhen, Chengdu, and Shanghai, with travel times ranging from a few hours to overnight depending on your departure point. The main high-speed hub is , while other stations serve different routes. You can book the ticket in advance here.

After taking the train from Guangzhou to Guilin, you will arrive at . From there, you can take public bus #22 to the city center for 2 CNY, which takes about an hour, or simply hail a Didi to go directly to your hotel.

After spending several days in Guilin, we will head to , the capital of Guizhou, one of China’s most underrated regions. We’ll use  as a base to explore the area’s stunning natural landscapes and rich cultural sites. The city itself is not particularly remarkable, but its slightly gritty character offers a glimpse of what life is like in a typical non-first-tier city in China.
From Guiyang, you can take a high-speed train from  to Anshunxi Railway Station, which should take about 30 minutes and cost 47 CNY. You can book the train here.

Afterwards, you can continue along the cliffside path, taking in stunning views such as the Five Ladies Expedition Rocks and many others. One of my favorite viewpoints here is the , which inspired the dreamy landscapes in the movie Avatar. The solitary vertical pillar rising amidst the picturesque scenery is truly a sight to behold.

The main highlight of Tianzi Mountain is , which offers several incredible viewpoints where you can see some of the mountain’s most unique formations. One of the most famous is the Imperial Brush Peak, a pillar that resembles the tip of a paintbrush and looks especially surreal in the soft morning light.

Along the way, you will also stop at , home to the Yao ethnic minority. The village is famous for its well-preserved stilt houses and traditional wooden architecture. Interestingly, it is also the birthplace of the woman with the longest hair in China, who holds the Guinness World Record with hair measuring 5.627 meters (18 ft 5.54 in) as of May 2004.

From Middle Temple, you can continue for another 15 minutes to reach the , a larger rest stop located just before the Five Colored Pool. It offers a much better view of the surrounding mountains compared to Middle Temple.

To get here, you can take a train from Chengdu East Railway Station to , which takes about two hours. Be sure to book your train tickets together with your Jiuzhaigou entrance ticket at least three days in advance, as both sell out very quickly. You do not want to secure your park ticket but then find that the trains are fully booked, leaving you without a way to get there and back.

By Train: One of the most popular ways to get to Huanglong is to take a high-speed train from Chengdu. You can take a train from Chengdu East Railway Station to , which takes about two hours. Be sure to book your train tickets together with your Jiuzhaigou entrance ticket at least three days in advance if you are planning to go to Jiuzhaigou as well, as both sell out very quickly. You do not want to secure your park ticket but then find that the trains are fully booked, leaving you without a way to get there and back.

The main highlight of Tianzi Mountain is Helong Park, which offers several incredible viewpoints where you can see some of the mountain’s most unique formations. One of the most famous is the , a pillar that resembles the tip of a paintbrush and looks especially surreal in the soft morning light.
With the ticket I recommended, it also includes a ticket to see , an outdoor one-hour large-scale show performed by the local Naxi people, set against the dramatic backdrop of Yulong Snow Mountain.

This Bai ethnic village is located remotely in the mountains, which is why it still retains its authenticity. The only way to get there is by road, which is about 2-3 hours by car from Dali Ancient Town or 4-5 hours by public buses and minivan. These leave from Dali North Bus Station, and you will have to transfer to a minivan in .

Another must-visit historical district in Chengdu is . It is a more stylized, newly renovated area featuring beautiful lanterns and lights, plenty of recreational spaces such as a square with outdoor restaurants, live music, and more. The street is more focused on street food and shops rather than historic buildings, unlike Kuanzhai Alley. Both districts are worth visiting in my opinion.
From Guiyang, you can take a high-speed train from Guiyang North Railway Station to , which should take about 30 minutes and cost 58 CNY. You can book the train here.

You can come to the harbour about an hour before sunset and go check out some of the tourist attractions nearby like the , one of Hong Kong's iconic landmarks. Once you are done, you can go up the elevated bridge over the pier, find a place to sit, and enjoy the wonderful light shows at sunset.
Another great place to hang out at night is , one of the best-preserved traditional street districts in Chengdu, located in the Qingyang District not far from Tianfu Square.

That said, like many places in China, Xijiang Village has become somewhat commercialized, and some travelers prefer smaller, more authentic villages such as  or Rongjiang. Even so, I still believe Xijiang is one of the most beautiful Miao villages and well worth a visit.

Probably one of the most beautiful viewpoints in Guilin,  is a steep karst mountain located right next to Xingping Ancient Village. You can hike up to see the stunning Li River winding around the jagged peaks of Guilin's karst landscape.

Another excellent day trip from Chengdu is a visit to the , one of Sichuan’s most iconic landmarks. The massive Buddha statue stands over 71 meters tall, making it the largest in the world, and is carved directly into a cliff face at the confluence of the Min, Dadu, and Qingyi Rivers.

From Oriental Buddha Park, you should be able to hike straight to , which is connected via a set of stairs that you will have to hike to get to. It's very long, and you should arrive at the viewpoint where you can see  from above in no time.

By High-Speed Train: High-speed trains depart from Chengdu East, Chengdu South, and Chengdu West Stations, arriving at . Once you arrive, you can take a local bus or taxi to the Leshan Giant Buddha Scenic Area. A Didi ride takes about 20 minutes, while the bus takes around 40 minutes depending on traffic.

is the heart of the city and one of the best-preserved ancient towns in China. With its cobbled streets, traditional Naxi architecture, and flowing canals, it feels like you are stepping back in time. The town has a maze-like layout, full of narrow alleyways, small bridges, and quaint courtyards, and you never know what beautiful hidden corners lie beyond them. It’s truly a wonderful place to explore.

By Train: Lijiang is also connected by high-speed trains. The most popular routes are from Kunming, which takes around three hours, from Dali, which takes one to one and a half hours, and from Shangri-La, which takes about one hour. Trains arrive at , located about 15 to 20 minutes from Old Town and you can get a Didi to your accommodation. You can book the train here.

By Plane:  is the main gateway into the city. Direct flights operate from major Chinese cities such as Kunming, Chengdu, Chongqing, Guangzhou, Beijing, and Shanghai. From the airport, it takes about 30 to 40 minutes to reach Lijiang Old Town by taxi or DiDi. There is also an airport shuttle bus that runs to Lijiang Old Town and Blue Sky Hotel.

Once you arrive back at the top, you can spend the rest of your time exploring the temples and shrines around Giant Leshan Buddha like the beautiful , Lingyun Temple, and more. You can also go and take a cruise along the river to see the Giant Leshan Buddha from the river, if you have the time.

Once you arrive back at the top, you can spend the rest of your time exploring the temples and shrines around Giant Leshan Buddha like the beautiful Lingbao Pagoda, , and more. You can also go and take a cruise along the river to see the Giant Leshan Buddha from the river, if you have the time.

Last but not least, be sure to visit the , located in the Shawan area just outside the East Gate of Fenghuang Ancient Town. The palace faces the river and is backed by hills, and you will immediately notice its iconic red building behind a tall pagoda as you enter the ancient town.

Last but not least, if you are looking for a great day trip to take from Guilin City, you are going to love . The place is another iconic attraction that is located about 2 - 3 hours away from Guilin City, with its beautiful rice paddies climbing up the slopes of the karst mountains in intricate layers.

Once you arrive at the pier, your guide will purchase the ticket for you, and you can board the boat according to the boat and seat number provided. The  is another beautiful waterfall you will encounter while hiking up the trail. It is located at around 3,240 to 3,270 meters above sea level, spans 167 meters in length, 19 meters in width, and has a vertical drop of 25 meters.
Once you arrive at , you will be greeted by beautiful open grasslands where horses and cattle roam freely, with the snow-capped peaks of Yading towering all around.

The fort is surrounded by a beautiful park, and it’s just a short hike up from the Ruins of Saint Paul’s before you reach its formidable gate, making it a perfect stop after visiting the ruins. Inside the fort, you’ll also find the , where you can learn all about the fort’s history as you explore.
Since this itinerary begins in Macau, you can either fly directly to , which is served by airlines like AirAsia, which has great coverage in Asia.

From Macau, you can take a high-speed ferry from the  to the Hong Kong Macau Ferry Terminal. From the city center, you can catch bus #3A to the , which takes about 25 minutes and costs 5 MOP/HKD. The ferry costs around 200 HKD and takes just under an hour. You can book your ticket in advance here.

In the afternoon, you can continue south, and you will find , a historic residential complex, once home to Zheng Guanying, a prominent 19th-century Chinese scholar and reformist, where you can stroll around and admire its beautiful decorations and artifacts.

One of the reasons I recommend choosing Route B over the others is the stunning view from the , especially if you arrive early in the morning. I highly recommend going early to avoid crowds and enjoy the best visibility, as fog tends to build up later in the day, especially during summer.

After about two hours of hiking, you should arrive at the , an inaccessible temple that also serves as a rest stop. You can take a break here, as there are many seating areas and a few shops selling snacks to keep you going. Since it is so close to Huanglong Temple, the next stop, I suggest you push through this rest stop and rest at Huanglong Temple for a better view of the place.

Next, you will find the , a smooth and beautiful pool located around 3,400 meters above sea level. The water is so calm and still that it earned the name , and you will find a collection of over 180 pools in this area.

Located right next to the Ruins of Saint Paul's, you will find , a historic fortress built by the Portuguese in the early 17th century on top of a hill overlooking the entire old town area to protect Macau from invasions. Be sure to go and hike up to it in the morning when it is not too hot.

By Shuttle Service from Jiuzhaigou: If you decide to explore Jiuzhaigou on the first day and want to get to Huanglong from there, you can book a shuttle service here under "Jiuzhaigou- Huanglong Attraction Shared Transfer". It costs around 8 USD per person one-way, with departures from 7 AM to 1 PM almost every hour, usually from . Once you book, the travel agency will send you the exact pickup location and vehicle license plate via WeChat, so keep an eye out for the message.

The night before, I booked a shuttle service (the one listed under “Jiuzhaigou –Huanglong Attraction Shared Transfer”) for 8 USD. Around 9 PM, they sent me the pickup time, location, and the bus license plate. The next morning, I woke up early, headed to the pickup point at , and caught the 7 AM bus to Huanglong.

Getting Jiuzhaigou to Huanglong: After you are done exploring Jiuzhaigou on the first day, you can book a shuttle service here under "Jiuzhaigou-Huanglong Attraction Shared Transfer" and take it to Huanglong the next morning. One of my favorite places in Lijiang Old Town has got to be , once the seat of the Mu family, the hereditary Naxi chieftains who ruled Lijiang for about 470 years through the Yuan, Ming, and Qing dynasties. It stands at the foot of Lion Mountain right at the center of Lijiang Old Town, and its architecture reflects not just Naxi traditions but also strong Han‑style palace design.
After you are done here, you can take the metro and go visit , a beautiful Chinese garden with stunning temples located in the Wong Tai Sin District that feels more like Japan than Hong Kong.

When I visited, the lights were turned on around 6:30 PM, and I found the best view was , overlooking the entire town. People start gathering here and throughout the town to watch the lights come on, so be sure to be ready when it happens.

Beyond the murals, Baisha is also a living museum of Naxi culture. Its ancient streets are lined with traditional wooden and stone houses, old temples, and quiet courtyards. In the past, Baisha was a center for silk embroidery and traditional weaving, and nowadays you can visit the  to see skilled artisans at work.

Some of the best views in the village are not found on the main street but along the river. If you want to explore this area, I highly recommend starting and ending at either  or No. 7 Wind & Rain Bridge. These are stunning old bridges with unique architectural designs, located at both ends of the village.

To go boating along the Tuojiang River, you can book your ticket here under "Boat ticket." You can choose a day cruise for 76 CNY or a night cruise for 138 CNY, and board from either the  or the Wanshou Wharf. The ride takes about 25 minutes. The day cruise runs from 8 AM to 5:50 PM, while the night cruise operates from 6:30 PM to 11 PM.

is one of Jiuzhaigou’s most iconic sights and also one of the most crowded areas in the park. Located at an elevation of about 2,365 meters, it spans 320 meters in width and plunges roughly 24.5 meters. Its Chinese name comes from Tibetan, meaning “majestic and splendid”, which perfectly describes how I felt when I first saw it in person.

As I mentioned earlier, Lijiang Old Town is massive, and I almost missed visiting the , located right at the main entrance. I know 😅. In my defense, I didn’t enter from that side since my hotel was on the north side. It’s amazing to think that I nearly missed this beautiful town square, where many of the town’s events are held!

For this itinerary, we are going to spend half a day not only visiting the Giant Leshan Buddha but also exploring , a lush green park full of incredibly beautiful and stunning rock-carving Buddha statues scattered across the mountains. This is where we will start this itinerary before we head over to the Giant Leshan Buddha.

From Leshan Train Station, you can use Didi or a taxi to reach the , where you can purchase a combined ticket for both the Oriental Buddha Park and the Giant Leshan Buddha. The total cost is around 160 CNY.

sits at around 2,587 m elevation and is another mirrored turquoise lake that is reachable on foot from Arrow Bamboo Lake so you do not need to take another bus to get to. You can stroll along its lakeside trail and it should take you all the way to  Waterfall, another must-see here.

By Cycling: Once you are near Erhai Lake, you can rent a bicycle from tourist hotspots around the lake like  and you can ride along its bank and enjoy the scenery. It should cost around 30-40 CNY to rent a bike for a day here.

After you have explored the beautiful Chonggu Temple, you can begin the hike to , which is the lowest holy lake in Yading at 4,100 meters above sea level and 0.75 acres formed by the melting snow on Xiannairi Mountain.

You can also attend the "Beautiful Xijiang" show to see many of these performances in one place at the . The show takes place every day at 11 AM, and ticket prices range from 80 to 150 CNY per person, depending on your seating choice in the theatre.

One of the best day trips you can take from Shangri-La is to visit , also known as Pudacuo National Park. This breathtaking nature reserve is located about 22 km northeast of Shangri-La at an elevation of roughly 3,500 meters, where beautiful alpine lakes, wetlands, forests, and grasslands are waiting for you to explore.

One of the most iconic landmarks you will see right away the moment you arrive at Dukezong Ancient Town is the massive gold-colored  located on top of Turtle Hill, right next to Dafo Temple. After Shuzheng Village, you can stop by at , a beautiful lake known for its dense beds of reeds that sway gently in the wind. A clear, jade-colored brook, which is often called the “Jade Ribbon”, meanders through the reeds and divides the lake into two halves.

Right around the corner of the Three Pagoda, you will find the  where you can get that epic shot of the Three Pagodas reflecting on the calm water of the pool. Be sure to go there and take some photos before continuing to explore Chongsheng Temple Complex.

That said, like many places in China, Xijiang Village has become somewhat commercialized, and some travelers prefer smaller, more authentic villages such as Langde or . Even so, I still believe Xijiang is one of the most beautiful Miao villages and well worth a visit.

is probably one of the most famous landmarks in Shangri-La and it is one of my favorite places in the city. This beautiful monastery sits about 5 km north from Shangri-La at an elevation of roughly 3,380 meters and it is considered the largest Tibetan Buddhist monastery in Yunnan, so much so that some people often refer to it as the "Little Potala Palace". This is as close to Tibet as you can get without having to go on an expensive organized tour.

To get here, you will first have to take a Didi taxi to the  and then you can get the ticket at the ticket office for 75 CNY per person. The ticket should also include the shuttle bus taking you to and from the monastery entrance. Once you have the ticket, you can proceed to the shuttle bus and it will take you there in 10 minutes.

You can go for a stroll and explore the surrounding streets which should lead to other tourist attractions in the area like , a stunning pastel-yellow Baroque-style church, Cathedral of the Nativity of Our Lady, and more.

is a large open plaza surrounded by government buildings, shopping streets, and cultural sites. At its center stands the iconic Statue of Mao Zedong, surrounded by colorful flower beds and artistic sculptures, all beautifully illuminated by the neon lights of the city.

is a large, themed film set and tourist attraction located right next to the Ximatan Cable Car Station to Cangshan Mountain, which makes for a great place to drop by and check out after you are done hiking the mountain. It was originally built for the filming of the famous Jin Yong (Louis Cha) martial arts drama Demi‑Gods and Semi‑Devils.

You can take the longer route first, which loops around the hill toward  and leads you past several scenic spots, including the Glass Walkways, the suspension bridge, and plenty of stunning viewpoints along the way. We will be spending 2 full days in Hong Kong, and so on the first day you arrive, you can leave your bags at the hotel and go explore , a vibrant shopping district filled with casual eateries and eclectic local shops along neon-lit Nathan Road, as well as luxury malls lining nearby Canton Road. Not far from Snow Bridge, you will find the , a unique crossing that connects the two sides of the river with two rows of independent square stone piers. True to its name, crossing the river requires stepping from one stone to the next, with the crystal-clear Tuojiang River flowing beneath your feet, making it both fun and scenic.

Once evening rolls around, come back to  and watch the city lit up in dazzling lights as the sun sets behind the horizon. Hong Kong's skyline is legendary, and there’s no better place to enjoy it than here.

One of the first experiences you will witness is the Welcome Wine-Drinking Ceremony, also called "Twelve Rounds of Welcome Wine", held at the . It begins around 10:30 AM in the front courtyard. I just so happened to arrive around that time and was able to see the Miao people performing music with traditional instruments and dances in their full attire.

is one of the oldest and most important Buddhist temples in the city, dating back more than 1,400 years to the Sui Dynasty (around 605–617). It has been destroyed and rebuilt in different styles throughout its history, but nowadays, the temple’s architecture is typical of classical Chinese Buddhist temples, with a sequence of halls aligned along a central axis, leading to the main Buddha hall and the sutra library.
From Hong Kong, you can take a high-speed train from  to Guangzhou South Railway Station. It costs around 30 USD and takes a little over an hour. You can book the ticket in advance here.

is one of the most iconic viewpoints along the Li River. From the top, you can see the river bend in a dramatic horseshoe shape, surrounded by countless karst peaks stretching into the distance, and it is one of my favorite viewpoints in Guilin.

While you are exploring the lower part of the village, you might also want to go and check out , a small local museum located not too far from the Lusheng Field, one of the main squares in the village. The museum consists of several rooms spread out across 2 floors.

If you prefer a more rustic feel of an ancient town, yet still want beauty and charm, I highly recommend visiting . You can then compare it with Dali Ancient Town and see which one you like best. Personally, I enjoy  during the day and Dali Ancient Town at night.

The bus stopped at a small roadside rest stop for a toilet break about an hour before arriving. The toilet is as basic as it gets, just holes in the ground and no doors, so be prepared. There’s also a small fruit stall where you can buy some snacks for the ride. After about 6.5 hours, I arrived at  around 2:30 PM, exhausted but physically intact.

After about 6.5 hours, I arrived at  around 2:30 PM, exhausted but physically intact. Toward the end of the trail, you will come across . While it’s not very large, the volume of water and the proximity you can get, since the walking trail hugs the cliffside above the drop, make it feel even more impressive than the larger Doupotang Waterfall. There are three main stops inside the park, with the first being the , a riverside walking path. While it may not feature any major attractions, it is a peaceful way to escape the crowds and offers the chance to spot yaks and horses along the route.

After exploring Yangjiajie, you can take another shuttle bus to , which is arguably the most scenic area of the park and offers much more to see and do compared to other parts. If you are short on time, you could even skip Tianzi and Yangjiajie altogether and focus solely on .

Let's start with one of the most epic attractions and one of my personal favorites, , also known as Jade Dragon Snow Mountain. Rising to 5,596 meters, it is one of Yunnan's most iconic natural landmarks, and its dramatic peak can often be seen from Lijiang on a clear day.

You can take the longer route first, which loops around the hill toward Tianmen Temple and leads you past several scenic spots, including the Glass Walkways, the suspension bridge, and plenty of stunning viewpoints along the way. The highest point on Tianmen Mountain is , which rises to 1,518.6 meters above sea level. This makes it a perfect vantage point to take in the entire upper part of Tianmen Mountain, and it is definitely an amazing spot to visit.

The journey took about 45 minutes, passing through Yading Village and ending at . From there, I walked about 20 minutes to reach Chonggu Grasslands and the temple, where you will find a beautiful Tibetan temple and a hiking trail leading to Pearl Lake, another scenic attraction.

As you continue up the mountain, you will come across the , a collection of over 658 colorful pools situated at an altitude of 3,454 meters. It is the second-largest pool cluster in Huanglong Valley and one of my favorites, with its stunning orange-colored travertine contrasting perfectly against the snowy forest and mountains in the background. Be sure to stop here and take photos before continuing!

From Yunnan's Shangri-La City, you will have to go to the  the day prior to get the physical ticket. There should be 2 buses leaving in the morning and the afternoon, and it should cost around 148 CNY. I got the one at 8:20 AM, but there should also be one at around 2 PM.

Since Huanglong is best visited together with Jiuzhaigou as part of a two-day, one-night trip, and there is no accommodation near Huanglong itself, I suggest staying overnight in Jiuzhaigou. If you spend the first day exploring Huanglong, you can take a shuttle bus to Jiuzhaigou, which will drop you off at a  near the hotels. Here are some recommended accommodations in Jiuzhaigou:

You will be dropped off at a  located right next to the entrance of Jiuzhaigou Scenic Area. Most of the hotels I recommend are in this area as well, so you can check in first and it is only a few minutes’ walk to the park entrance.

By Scenic Buses: For day trips to Potatso National Park or Balagezong Grand Canyon, you can go with an organized scenic bus which you can book from here. Once you have the bus booked, you will have to go exchange it at the scenic  and they will direct you to the bus you have to take. Very easy and convenient.

Getting there is also very easy, especially if you are already at Zhangjiajie City as you can easily catch a cable car from the  right in the city center, and it will take you about 40 minutes to get to the top. First and foremost, you will have to reserve your ticket first, which will define which route you will have to take to get up there.

One of the highest areas you can explore to acclimatize yourself with the high altitude of Dali is Cangshan Mountain, a majestic mountain range rising over 4,000 meters above sea level, located just west of Dali City. You can easily access it via the .

By Boat: Another great way to see Fenghuang from a different perspective is via boat on the Tuojiang River. You can admire the beautiful stilt houses, old wooden houses, bridges, and the riverside skyline from the river, which is especially lovely in the late afternoon or at night when lanterns and lights reflect on the water. You should be able to .
You will be dropped off at the , and you can either begin hiking or take the shuttle bus to get to the cable car up to Huanglong Scenic Area.

When I visited, the lights were turned on around 6:30 PM, and I found the best view was , overlooking the entire town. People start gathering here and throughout the town to watch the lights come on, so be sure to be ready when it happens.

After you enter the Oriental Buddhist Park, you can walk across the bridge and look up at the mountain to your left and you will find a  carved out of the mountain. I almost missed it because it was a little foggy when I was there, but apparently, it is the largest stone reclining Buddha image in the region.

To take the cable car up, you will need to buy a ticket that includes the cable car at its service center. The service center is accessible from the park entrance via a free shuttle bus, which you can catch . The cable car ride up costs 80 CNY, and the ride down costs 40 CNY, in addition to the standard park entrance fee of 170 CNY. Once you arrive at the upper station, you can either walk about 20 minutes or take a quick electric shuttle service for 15 CNY per person to take you to the trailhead to Five-Colored Pool.

Due to the heavy foot traffic, Elephant Trunk Hill is only accessible from one gate, , and visitors must walk south toward the exit. Make sure you enter through the correct gate. The place is open from 7 AM to 9:30 PM daily, and entry is free as long as you show your passport, so don't forget to bring it with you.

You will likely arrive at the top around the same time, and you will have already seen all the beautiful sights along the way. You can take the cable car down if you prefer, as there is usually no queue at the top. The trailhead is  and you can begin hiking immediately after you arrive. It should take about 2.5 hours before you arrive at the Five-Colored Pool.

By E-Scooter: If you want to get around Yangshuo, you can rent an e-scooter for about 50 to 60 CNY per day, usually available until 6 PM when the rental shops close, with a deposit of 200 CNY. I rented mine from one of the local rental spots (), and everything went smoothly.

place: lower station of the Bailong Elevator
description: Once you hike down to the river, make sure you follow the river east so that you will end up near the  and you can take the shuttle bus back to the East Gate. Otherwise, you might end up at the South Gate and you will have to find a bus to take you back to Wulingyuan yourself.

It's not a must-stop, but if you have time to spare and you want to be around peaceful nature, it is worth it. The trail takes about 45 minutes before you arrive at a  where you can catch the shuttle bus to the next stop.

The cruise often takes about 6 hours and you can book it in advance here or through your accommodation. The tour should pick you up at 7 or 8 AM at your hotel in Guilin City, and you will be transferred to the , which will take about an hour.

place: place at the corner of Yangshuo West Street
description: To get to Ruyi Peak, you will first have to rent an e-scooter, which you can do so from the  for about 60 CNY per day. Once you've got your vehicle, you can drive south across the river for about 45 minutes before you arrive at the cable car station at the foot of Ruyi Peak.

To get to and from Potatso National Park, you will need to take the scenic shuttle bus service, which you can book here. The shuttle costs around 243 CNY per person. Once you receive your email receipt, you must exchange it at the , where the staff will direct you to the correct bus to the park. Buses depart from 8:40 AM to 1:00 PM at roughly 30-minute intervals, and return trips from Potatso leave after 1 PM from the same location they drop you off, every 30 minutes.

To take the cable car up, you will need to buy a ticket that includes the cable car at its . The  is accessible from the park entrance via a free shuttle bus, which you can catch here. The cable car ride up costs 80 CNY, and the ride down costs 40 CNY, in addition to the standard park entrance fee of 170 CNY. Once you arrive at the upper station, you can either walk about 20 minutes or take a quick electric shuttle service for 15 CNY per person to take you to the trailhead to Five-Colored Pool.

After crossing the bridge to the next peak, you’ll find a  where you can pay your respects and enjoy the viewpoints along the walking path that wraps around the sandstone peak. The path forms a loop, allowing you to circle the peak and continue on to the next viewpoint.

The shuttle bus will drop you off at Tianqiao Bus Stop and from there, you can begin exploring Yuanjiajie Mountain starting with the Greatest Natural Bridge, a rocky natural bridge that connects 2 mountain peaks together. On the peak, you will also find a  where you can pay respect and enjoy all the viewpoints around the area.

You can go and check out , a famous promenade that runs along the Tsim Sha Tsui waterfront which was modeled after the Hollywood Walk of Fame. It celebrates Hong Kong’s rich film industry and honors legendary actors, directors, and filmmakers who have contributed to the city’s cinematic history.

Of course, one of the biggest draws of Macau is its luxurious casinos and larger-than-life entertainment, featuring Las Vegas–style replicas of famous landmarks from around the world but with their own glamorous twists. Most of them are concentrated around , which is where I recommend you visit after sundown.

Another cool attraction in Shangri-La is , located about a 10-minute taxi ride south of Dukezong Ancient Town. If you travel to Shangri-La by train, you may even catch a glimpse of them along the way.

On the day of your arrival, you can explore the area around your hotel, get your bearings, and relax before we begin the next day with a visit to one of the finest remnants of the Portuguese presence here, which is , probably the most iconic landmark in the city.

After a series of escalators from the Gateway to Heaven, you will arrive at , where the entire mountain opens up to you with countless hiking opportunities, along with plenty of amenities like restaurants, street food stalls, and cafes to keep you fueled and ready for all the adventures ahead.

Route C: For this route, you will be taking the bus both up and down the mountain, and you’ll need to backtrack between the Middle Station and the Upper Station. Essentially, you take the bus to , ride the new cable car up to the Middle Station, explore the area, then continue up to the Upper Station via the escalators. After exploring the Upper Station, you’ll return the same way you came.

If you insist on taking the cable car, you can catch the free shuttle bus from  to the lower station and buy the ticket from there. It should cost about 80 CNY per person going up and 40 CNY per person coming down. From the upper cable car station, you can either walk about 20 minutes or take an e-shuttle for 15 CNY, and it will take you to the trailhead where you can begin the rest of the hike to Five-Colored Pool. After you are done, you can make your way down on foot to see the remaining attractions.

The fare was 51 CNY per person, and the ride took about two hours before arriving at , right next to the Jiuzhaigou entrance. By the time I arrived, it was already 12:35 PM, so I went straight to Jiuzhaigou and spent the next 4-5 hours exploring the park. If you take the 6:15 AM train, you will have 6-7 hours, which is ideal, so you do not have to rush as I did.

The hike starts from  shown in the photo above, and you should be able to walk there from Xingping Ancient Village. From the trailhead, the hike should take about 30 minutes without rest up an extremely steep trail.

The next morning, I woke up around 6 AM and walked to the main road by 6:30 AM to find a taxi to Yading Nature Reserve. After about five minutes, I found a car-pool taxi. The driver asked where I was headed and invited me to hop on with other tourists, and I was on my way to the reserve. The ride took only about 15 minutes and cost 10 CNY per person, dropping us off at the  at the entrance.
